comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the proposal for a virtual file system (VFS) in Node.js, with mixed opinions on its usefulness, security implications, and implementation challenges. Some commenters highlight existing solutions and ecosystem issues, while others express concerns about AI-generated code and project direction.

comments.author: Normal_gaussian
comments.text:
yarn pnp is currently broken on Node v25.7+;

- https://github.com/yarnpkg/berry/issues/7065

- https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/62012

This is because yarn patches fs in order to introduce virtual file path resolution of modules in the yarn cache (which are zips), which is quite brittle and was broken by a seemingly unrelated change in 25.7.

The discussion in issue 62012 is notable - it was suggested yarn just wait for vfs to land. This is interesting to me in two ways: firstly, the node team seems quite happy for non-trivial amounts of the ecosystem to just be broken, and suggests relying on what I'm assuming will be an experimental API when it does land; secondly, it implies a lot of confidence that this feature will land before LTS.
comments.analysis: This comment provides concrete links to issues and explains a real compatibility problem, reflecting a deep understanding of the ecosystem and the implications of VFS integration.

comments.author: mohsen1
comments.text:
Yarn, pnpm, webpack all have solutions for this. Great to see this becoming a standard. I have a project that is severely handicapped due to FS. Running 13k tests takes 40 minutes where a virtual file system that Node would just work with it would cut the run time to 3 minutes. I experimented with some hacks and decided to stay with slow but native FS solution.

What I really want is a way of swapping FS with VFS in a Node.js program harness. Something like

     node --use-vfs --vfs-cache=BIG_JSON_FILE
So basically Node never touches the disk and load everything from the memory
comments.analysis: The comment accurately notes existing tooling and the potential performance benefits of a VFS, supporting the argument with practical experience.

comments.author: wccrawford
comments.text:
I'm not convinced that allowing Node to import "code generated at runtime" is actually a good thing. I think it should have to go through the hoops to get loaded, for security reasons.

I like the idea of it mocking the file system for tests, but I feel like that should probably be part of the test suite, not Node.

The example towards the end that stores data in a sqlite provider and then saves it as a JSON file is mind-boggling to me. Especially for a system that's supposed to be about not saving to the disk. Perhaps it's just a bad example, but I'm really trying to figure out how this isn't just adding complexity.
comments.analysis: Raises valid security concerns and questions the scope of Node's responsibilities, emphasizing good software design principles.

comments.author: indutny
comments.text: Taking the question of whether this would be a useful addition to Node.js core or aside, it must be noted that this 19k LoC PR was mostly generated by Claude Code and manually reviewed by the submitter which in my opinion is against the spirit of the project and directly violates the terms of Developer's Certificate of Origin set in the project's CONTRIBUTING.md
comments.analysis: Highlights important ethical and procedural concerns about AI-generated code contributions, relevant to open source governance.

comments.author: moralestapia
comments.text:
>Let me be honest: a PR that size would normally take months of full-time work. This one happened because I built it with Claude Code.

The node.js codebase and standard library has a very high standard of quality, hope that doesn't get washed out by sloppy AI-generated code.

OTOH, Matteo is an excellent engineer and the community owes a lot to him. So I guess the code is solid :).
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced perspective on AI-assisted development, acknowledging both risks and the submitter's reputation.

comments.author: notnullorvoid
comments.text: I could see something like this being useful if it could be passed to workers to replace any fs access inside the worker.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical use case for VFS integration, showing understanding of Node's worker threads and modularity.

comments.author: ozlikethewizard
comments.text: I'm not convinced this needs to be in core Node, but being able to have serverless functions access a file system without providing storage would definitely have some use cases. Had some fun with video processing recently that this would be perfect for.
comments.analysis: Reasonably questions core inclusion while recognizing niche use cases, showing balanced consideration.

comments.author: rigorclaw
comments.text: the multi-tenant sandboxing use case is interesting but mount() being process-global feels like a footgun. has anyone explored per-isolate or per-worker VFS scoping? seems like the kind of thing that would matter a lot for platform companies running untrusted code.
comments.analysis: Insightfully points out architectural concerns relevant to security and multi-tenant environments.

comments.author: PaulHoule
comments.text: Would be nice if node packages could be packed up in ZIP files so to avoid the security/metadata tax for small file access on Windows.
comments.analysis: Suggests a practical optimization related to file packaging and performance on Windows.

comments.author: petcat
comments.text: Are people still building new projects on Node.js?  I would have thought the ecosystem was moving to deno or bun now
comments.analysis: Raises a relevant question about ecosystem trends, though somewhat speculative and less directly related to VFS.

comments.author: austin-cheney
comments.text:
Most of the 4 justifications mentioned sound like mitigations of otherwise bad design decisions. JavaScript in the browser went down this path for the longest time where new standards were introduced only to solve for stupid people instead of actually introducing new capabilities that were otherwise unachievable.

I do see some original benefits to a VFS though, bad application decisions aside, but they are exceedingly minor.

As an aside I think JavaScript would benefit from an in-memory database. This would be more of language enhancement than a Node.js enhancement. Imagine the extended application capabilities of an object/array store native to the language that takes queries using JS logic to return one or more objects/records. No SQL language and no third party databases for stuff that you don't want to keep in offline storage on a disk.
comments.analysis: Offers a critical perspective on the proposal and suggests an alternative enhancement, though somewhat subjective.

comments.author: adzm
comments.text: How does electron do this with its packaged files? I suppose it does not work with module resolution?
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question about Electron's approach, but does not provide analysis or evidence.

comments.author: mg
comments.text:
You can’t import or require() a module
    that only exists in memory.
You can convert it into a data url and import that, can't you?
comments.analysis: Provides a technically accurate workaround for importing in-memory modules, adding useful information.

comments.author: westurner
comments.analysis: Connects the VFS discussion to related projects, showing awareness of broader ecosystem but without direct evaluation.

comments.author: pier25
comments.text:
The Node team has lost the plot IMO.

By far the most critical issue is the over reliance on third party NPM packages for even fundamental needs like connecting to a database.
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration with Node.js ecosystem management but lacks constructive suggestions or evidence.
